notes.anchor: the seventeenth day before the Kalends of July
notes.note: The Kalends of July fell on the 1st; counted inclusively in the Roman manner, this dates the arrival at Actium to 15 June 51 BC.
The Salii, the leaping priests of Mars, were proverbial for the sumptuous banquets that followed their festival processions; "Salian fashion" (Saliarem in modum) is a stock phrase for a lavish feast.

notes.anchor: we carry this extraordinary command through
notes.note: Cicero's proconsulship of Cilicia (51--50 BC), reluctantly accepted under the lex Pompeia of 52 BC which required a five-year interval between magistracy and provincial governorship; he calls it "extraordinary" (extraordinarium) because it fell to him against his wishes.

notes.anchor: that our year stands at its present term
notes.note: Cicero begs Atticus to ensure his provincial command is held to a single year and not prorogued; he was anxious above all not to have his tenure of Cilicia extended.

The pre-Julian calendar fell out of step with the seasons, and the pontifices periodically inserted an intercalary month; an added month would lengthen Cicero's year of office, so he urges Atticus to oppose any such insertion.
